scenarios.title: Instagram Reels Analytics Pipeline
scenarios.description: Design a pipeline that tracks Reels views, shares, likes, and completion rates at Meta scale (500M daily active users). Must support: real-time dashboards (< 5 min latency), historical trend analysis, and A/B test measurement.
systemComponents: Raw Storage Layer (Data Lake, partitioned by date/hour)
keyConsiderations: Exactly-once semantics for view counts (prevent double-counting)
Completion rate requires start and end events to be matched
Late-arriving events (user finishes watching after connectivity gap)
Deduplication of events from at-least-once delivery systems
Partitioning strategy for both real-time and historical queries
A/B test assignment must be joined at query time, not baked in at ingestion
scenarios.solutionApproach: Events flow from Reels clients into Scribe (Meta's logging infrastructure). A Spark Streaming job reads from Scribe every 60 seconds, applies deduplication using event_id, validates the schema, and writes micro-batches to Hive tables partitioned by event_date and event_hour. Completion rate is computed by joining view_start and view_end events on (user_id, reel_id, session_id) within a 4-hour window to handle gaps. A separate hourly batch pipeline computes daily aggregates for historical analysis. A/B test variants are stored in a separate experiment assignment table and joined at query time in Presto. For real-time dashboards, a pre-aggregated summary table (updated every 5 minutes) is served via a fast-path query layer.
scenarios.pseudoCode:
-- Real-time aggregation query (runs every 5 min on Presto)
SELECT
  reel_id,
  creator_id,
  DATE_TRUNC('hour', event_timestamp) AS event_hour,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN event_type = 'view_start' THEN user_id END) AS unique_viewers,
  COUNT(CASE WHEN event_type = 'like' THEN 1 END) AS total_likes,
  COUNT(CASE WHEN event_type = 'share' THEN 1 END) AS total_shares,
  -- Completion rate: users who reached >= 90% of video
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN event_type = 'view_complete' THEN user_id END) * 1.0
    / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN event_type = 'view_start' THEN user_id END), 0)
    AS completion_rate
FROM reel_events
WHERE event_date = CURRENT_DATE
  AND event_hour >= DATE_TRUNC('hour', NOW()) - INTERVAL '1' HOUR
GROUP BY 1, 2, 3;

-- Deduplication at ingestion (Spark Streaming)
val deduplicated = rawEvents
  .withWatermark("event_timestamp", "10 minutes")
  .dropDuplicates(Seq("event_id"))
  .writeStream
  .format("hive")
  .option("checkpointLocation", "/checkpoints/reels")
  .partitionBy("event_date", "event_hour")
  .start()
scenarios.aiApproach: The key engineering challenge is matching view_start and view_end events for completion rate while handling late arrivals and at-least-once delivery. Use watermarking in Spark Structured Streaming to bound the state store and prevent unbounded memory growth. For A/B tests, never bake the experiment variant into the raw events — always join from the experiment assignment table at query time to allow retroactive experiment re-analysis.

scenarios.title: Ad Impression Tracking System
scenarios.description: Build a real-time ad impression and click tracking system for Facebook Ads. Requirements: 10 billion impressions per day, < 15 min data freshness for billing, idempotent processing, handle late clicks (up to 24 hours after impression).
systemComponents: Click Event Stream (separate topic, joined to impressions)
Idempotent Write Layer (partitioned Hive with OVERWRITE semantics)
Late Click Reconciliation Job (daily batch, looks back 24h)
keyConsiderations: Idempotency: re-running the pipeline must produce identical results (use event_id as dedup key)
Late clicks: clicks can arrive up to 24h after the impression — need a reconciliation strategy
Billing correctness: advertisers are billed on clicks, so missed or duplicate clicks have financial impact
Grain: one row per impression in the fact table, click columns nullable (NULL until clicked)
Partitioning: partition by impression_date, not click_date, to keep billing windows clean
Exactly-once delivery: stream processor must commit offsets only after successful write
scenarios.solutionApproach: Impression events stream into Scribe and are processed by Flink every minute, written to fact_ad_impressions partitioned by impression_date. The grain is one row per impression: (impression_id, ad_id, user_id, device_id, impression_timestamp, was_clicked=false, click_timestamp=null). A separate click stream processes click events within 5 minutes. For clicks arriving within the same micro-batch window, update the impression row inline. For late clicks (arriving up to 24h later), run a daily reconciliation job that scans the previous 2 days of impressions and joins to the clicks table — UPDATE was_clicked=true where a matching click is found. This ensures billing is always accurate within 24h. For idempotency: use UPSERT logic keyed on impression_id. The pipeline can be re-run safely.
scenarios.pseudoCode:
-- Fact table DDL
CREATE TABLE fact_ad_impressions (
  impression_id     BIGINT,
  ad_id             INT,
  campaign_id       INT,
  advertiser_id     INT,
  user_id           BIGINT,
  device_type       VARCHAR(20),
  impression_date   DATE,
  impression_ts     TIMESTAMP,
  was_clicked       BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
  click_ts          TIMESTAMP
)
PARTITIONED BY (impression_date)
STORED AS ORC;

-- Late click reconciliation (runs daily)
INSERT OVERWRITE TABLE fact_ad_impressions
PARTITION (impression_date)
SELECT
  i.impression_id,
  i.ad_id,
  i.campaign_id,
  i.advertiser_id,
  i.user_id,
  i.device_type,
  i.impression_date,
  i.impression_ts,
  CASE WHEN c.impression_id IS NOT NULL THEN TRUE ELSE i.was_clicked END AS was_clicked,
  COALESCE(c.click_ts, i.click_ts) AS click_ts
FROM fact_ad_impressions i
LEFT JOIN fact_ad_clicks c
  ON i.impression_id = c.impression_id
WHERE i.impression_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '2' DAY;

-- Billing aggregate query
SELECT
  advertiser_id,
  impression_date,
  COUNT(*) AS total_impressions,
  SUM(CASE WHEN was_clicked THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS total_clicks,
  SUM(CASE WHEN was_clicked THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) * 1.0 / COUNT(*) AS ctr
FROM fact_ad_impressions
WHERE impression_date = CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '1' DAY
GROUP BY advertiser_id, impression_date;
scenarios.aiApproach: The hardest part of this system is the late click reconciliation. Two strategies: (1) UPDATE the impression row when a late click arrives — requires a mutable storage format (Delta Lake, Hudi) which adds complexity. (2) Keep impressions and clicks in separate tables and join at query time for billing — simpler pipeline but slower billing queries. For Meta's scale, strategy 2 with a daily reconciliation rollup is standard: the billing query reads from a pre-aggregated daily table that is recomputed with the 24h lookback window.

scenarios.title: Data Quality Monitoring Framework
scenarios.description: Design a data quality monitoring framework for a pipeline ingesting 1PB/day. Must detect: schema drift, volume anomalies, null rate changes, and statistical distribution shifts. Alert within 10 minutes of detecting issues.
systemComponents: Profiling Job (runs after each pipeline batch, computes metrics)
Metrics Store (time-series DB: row count, null rates, min/max/p50/p95 per column)
Data Quality Dashboard (trend visualization per table/column)
keyConsiderations: Schema drift: new columns added, columns removed, type changes — compare current schema to registered schema
Volume anomalies: row counts should be within expected range (e.g., 3-sigma from rolling 30-day average)
Null rate changes: sudden spike in NULL rate for a critical column indicates upstream bug
Distribution shifts: use KL divergence or chi-square test on sampled data to detect categorical distribution changes
Alert fatigue: too many alerts is as bad as no alerts — set per-column thresholds with criticality levels
10-minute detection SLA: profiling must complete within the SLA window after data lands
scenarios.solutionApproach: After each pipeline batch writes to the warehouse, trigger a profiling job that: (1) Reads the schema from the catalog and compares to the registered baseline — alert on any column type change or removal, (2) Computes table statistics: row count, null rate per column, min/max/avg for numeric columns, cardinality for categorical columns, (3) Writes these metrics to a time-series metrics store, (4) Runs anomaly detection: for volume, flag if row count is outside mean +/- 3 standard deviations of the past 30 days. For null rates, flag if the current rate is > 2x the rolling average. For distributions, run a chi-square test on a 1% sample for high-cardinality categoricals. Alerts are routed to PagerDuty for critical tables and to Slack for non-critical. Each alert includes: table name, column, current value, expected range, and a link to the trend chart.
scenarios.pseudoCode:
-- Profiling query (runs after each pipeline batch)
INSERT INTO data_quality_metrics
SELECT
  CURRENT_TIMESTAMP AS check_timestamp,
  'fact_ad_impressions' AS table_name,
  'impression_date' AS partition_date,
  COUNT(*) AS row_count,
  -- Null rates per critical column
  AVG(CASE WHEN user_id IS NULL THEN 1.0 ELSE 0.0 END) AS user_id_null_rate,
  AVG(CASE WHEN ad_id IS NULL THEN 1.0 ELSE 0.0 END) AS ad_id_null_rate,
  AVG(CASE WHEN impression_ts IS NULL THEN 1.0 ELSE 0.0 END) AS ts_null_rate,
  -- Volume stats
  MIN(impression_ts) AS min_ts,
  MAX(impression_ts) AS max_ts
FROM fact_ad_impressions
WHERE impression_date = CURRENT_DATE;

-- Anomaly detection query
WITH RecentMetrics AS (
  SELECT
    row_count,
    AVG(row_count) OVER (ORDER BY check_timestamp ROWS BETWEEN 30 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING) AS rolling_avg,
    STDDEV(row_count) OVER (ORDER BY check_timestamp ROWS BETWEEN 30 PRECEDING AND 1 PRECEDING) AS rolling_std
  FROM data_quality_metrics
  WHERE table_name = 'fact_ad_impressions'
)
SELECT
  CASE
    WHEN ABS(row_count - rolling_avg) > 3 * rolling_std THEN 'CRITICAL: Volume anomaly'
    ELSE 'OK'
  END AS volume_status
FROM RecentMetrics
ORDER BY check_timestamp DESC
LIMIT 1;
scenarios.aiApproach: At 1PB/day, running a full table scan for every quality check is expensive. Use two strategies: (1) For partition-level checks (row counts, null rates), query only the latest partition — never do a full table scan. (2) For distribution checks, use sampling (TABLESAMPLE BERNOULLI(1)) to check 1% of data in under a minute. For schema drift, compare the Hive metastore schema directly without reading data at all — this is nearly free. In dbt, the tests (not_null, unique, accepted_values) implement a subset of this automatically and should be your first layer of defense.

scenarios.title: User Identity Graph Pipeline
scenarios.description: Design a pipeline that builds and maintains a user identity graph connecting multiple user identifiers (device IDs, email hashes, phone number hashes, cookie IDs) to a canonical user ID. The graph must be updated daily with 3 billion edges.
systemComponents: Edge Input Layer (daily batch of identifier pairs from various sources)
Graph Processing Engine (Spark GraphX or custom iterative SQL)
Change Detection Layer (which IDs changed canonical assignment)
keyConsiderations: Graph is dynamic: new edges are added daily, existing edges may be invalidated
Connected components must be computed efficiently at 3B edges — cannot fit in memory on one node
Canonical ID must be stable: once assigned, the same real-world user should keep the same canonical ID across runs
Merges: two previously separate identity clusters may be joined by a new edge — handle merge without invalidating all downstream joins
Splits: an identifier pair may be invalidated — previously merged cluster may need to be split
Privacy: identifier inputs are hashed — no raw PII stored in the pipeline
scenarios.solutionApproach: Build the graph pipeline in three stages. Stage 1: Ingest all identifier pairs for the day and union with the existing graph edges table. Stage 2: Run a distributed connected components algorithm using Spark GraphX or iterative SQL propagation — assign each connected component a component_id. Stage 3: Map component_id to canonical_user_id using a stable mapping table (first seen component_id becomes the canonical ID; on merge, the smaller canonical ID wins). Output: a fact_user_identity table with (identifier_type, identifier_hash, canonical_user_id, valid_from, valid_to). Run a change detection query to identify which canonical_user_ids changed and trigger downstream reprocessing for affected users.
scenarios.pseudoCode:
-- Stage 1: Build edge list
CREATE TABLE identity_edges AS
SELECT identifier_a, identifier_b, edge_type, edge_date
FROM new_edges_today
UNION ALL
SELECT identifier_a, identifier_b, edge_type, edge_date
FROM identity_edges_historical
WHERE is_valid = TRUE;

-- Stage 2: Iterative label propagation (simplified)
-- In production: use Spark GraphX ConnectedComponents
WITH RECURSIVE ComponentPropagation AS (
  -- Initialize: each node is its own component
  SELECT identifier, identifier AS component_id
  FROM all_identifiers
  UNION ALL
  -- Propagate: adopt the minimum component_id from neighbors
  SELECT e.identifier_b, MIN(cp.component_id)
  FROM ComponentPropagation cp
  JOIN identity_edges e ON cp.identifier = e.identifier_a
  GROUP BY e.identifier_b
  HAVING MIN(cp.component_id) < MAX(cp.component_id) -- Only continue if there's a change
)
SELECT identifier, MIN(component_id) AS final_component
FROM ComponentPropagation
GROUP BY identifier;

-- Stage 3: Map component to stable canonical ID
SELECT
  i.identifier,
  COALESCE(m.canonical_user_id, i.component_id) AS canonical_user_id
FROM computed_components i
LEFT JOIN canonical_id_mapping m ON i.component_id = m.component_id;
scenarios.aiApproach: The connected components problem is the core algorithmic challenge. Recursive SQL works for small graphs but does not scale to 3B edges. In production, use Spark GraphX ConnectedComponents or implement iterative message passing: each node sends its current minimum label to all neighbors, and nodes update their label if they receive a smaller value. Iterate until convergence (typically 10-20 iterations for social graphs). For stability, maintain a component_to_canonical mapping table that is append-only — new components get new canonical IDs, merges use the lower canonical ID.

scenarios.title: dbt-Based Incremental Pipeline Migration
scenarios.description: You are migrating 50 legacy stored procedures (running as nightly jobs on SQL Server) to a dbt-based pipeline on Snowflake. The legacy system has no tests, no documentation, and complex nested dependencies. Design the migration strategy.
systemComponents: Legacy System Audit (dependency mapping, row count baselines)
dbt Project Structure (staging, intermediate, marts layers)
Parallel Running Period (legacy + dbt in parallel for validation)
Test Coverage (dbt tests replacing manual QA)
keyConsiderations: Unknown dependencies: stored procedures may call each other in non-obvious ways — must map the full DAG before building dbt models
No test baseline: need to establish row count and business metric baselines from the legacy system before migration
Incremental vs. full refresh: identify which models can be incremental (saves cost) vs. which require full refresh
Cutover risk: business cannot tolerate missing data — need a parallel running validation period
Data type differences: SQL Server and Snowflake have different type systems — DATETIME vs TIMESTAMP_NTZ, etc.
Stakeholder communication: downstream BI tools and consumers must be notified of table rename/schema changes
scenarios.solutionApproach: Phase 1 — Audit (Week 1-2): Reverse-engineer the dependency DAG from stored procedure code using static analysis. Document inputs, outputs, and business logic for each procedure. Capture row count baselines and key business metrics from the current system. Phase 2 — Build dbt project (Weeks 3-6): Create staging models that read from the raw source (replicate the raw layer in Snowflake). Build intermediate models for complex transformations. Build mart models that match the legacy output schemas. Apply incremental materialization where the source has a reliable updated_at column. Phase 3 — Parallel running (Weeks 7-8): Run both legacy SQL Server and dbt pipelines simultaneously. Compare row counts, key aggregates, and sample records daily. Build an automated reconciliation query. Phase 4 — Cutover (Week 9): Switch BI tools to the Snowflake dbt mart tables. Decommission the legacy SQL Server jobs. Monitor for 2 weeks before final sign-off.
scenarios.pseudoCode:
-- dbt model: stg_orders.sql (staging layer)
{{ config(
  materialized='incremental',
  unique_key='order_id',
  on_schema_change='sync_all_columns'
) }}

SELECT
  order_id,
  customer_id,
  -- Normalize SQL Server DATETIME to Snowflake TIMESTAMP_NTZ
  CONVERT_TIMEZONE('UTC', order_date::TIMESTAMP_NTZ) AS order_date,
  order_amount,
  status,
  CURRENT_TIMESTAMP AS dbt_loaded_at
FROM {{ source('sql_server_raw', 'orders') }}
{% if is_incremental() %}
  WHERE order_date > (SELECT MAX(order_date) FROM {{ this }})
{% endif %}

-- Reconciliation query (run daily during parallel period)
SELECT
  'row_count' AS metric,
  (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM legacy_sql_server.dbo.fact_orders) AS legacy_value,
  (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM snowflake_dbt.marts.fact_orders) AS dbt_value,
  ABS((SELECT COUNT(*) FROM legacy_sql_server.dbo.fact_orders)
    - (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM snowflake_dbt.marts.fact_orders)) AS diff
UNION ALL
SELECT
  'total_revenue' AS metric,
  (SELECT SUM(order_amount) FROM legacy_sql_server.dbo.fact_orders) AS legacy_value,
  (SELECT SUM(order_amount) FROM snowflake_dbt.marts.fact_orders) AS dbt_value,
  ABS((SELECT SUM(order_amount) FROM legacy_sql_server.dbo.fact_orders)
    - (SELECT SUM(order_amount) FROM snowflake_dbt.marts.fact_orders)) AS diff;
scenarios.aiApproach: The parallel running period is the most critical risk mitigation step. Never do a hard cutover without at least 2 weeks of side-by-side validation. For the incremental strategy, prefer unique_key-based MERGE (dbt's default for Snowflake incremental models) over append-only — this handles late-arriving updates without requiring full refreshes. The hardest part is usually the data type normalization — SQL Server DATETIME is in local time with no timezone, while Snowflake TIMESTAMP_NTZ expects UTC. Build a macro to handle this conversion consistently across all staging models.
